preamble: The voiced labio-velar approximant /w/ can undergo vocalization, transforming into a diphthong, often one with a back rounded vowel component such as /au/. This shift typically occurs in specific phonetic environments, where the approximant loses its consonantal quality and merges with an adjacent vowel to form a diphthong.

examples.note: Old English hlǣw (meaning 'mound' or 'law') shifted to Middle English law, with /w/ vocalizing to form a diphthong after the back vowel.

sources: Hogg, R. M. (1992). A History of English Phonology. Routledge.
Phonological history of English diphthongs - Grokipedia.
